content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 37 (GEN_037)

## 1. Ketonet Passim — The Garment (vv. 3, 23, 32)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "coat of passim" (untranslated) | Passim genuinely uncertain; transliteration preserves Hebrew |
| LOGOS | "long ornamented robe" | Combines length (pas = palm/sole) with decoration (LXX poikilon) |
| PNEUMA | "special robe" | Captures distinction without specifying form |
| **SOPHIA** | **"ketonet passim [ornamented robe]" — new convention** | Parallels massebah treatment: Hebrew term preserved, bracketed gloss provides orientation. "Ornamented" supported by LXX and 2 Sam 13:18-19. |

## 2. Ba'al Ha-Chalomot — "Master of Dreams" vs. "Dreamer" (v. 19)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this master of dreams" | Literal: ba'al = master/owner |
| LOGOS | "that master of dreams" | Same; preserves the contemptuous tone |
| PNEUMA | "that dreamer" | Dynamic; simpler but loses the sarcasm of ba'al |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the master of dreams" — 2-1** | ba'al carries mockery — the brothers are sneering at Joseph's pretension to mastery over prophetic dreams. "Dreamer" is too neutral. |

## 3. Bor — "Pit" vs. "Cistern" (vv. 22, 24, 28, 29)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pit" | Basic meaning of bor; literary consistency with later prison imagery |
| LOGOS | "pit" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "cistern" | More specific; "no water in it" suggests a dry cistern |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Pit" — 2-1** | "Pit" preserves the literary foreshadowing: Joseph goes into the bor here and will later be in the bor of the prison (40:15, 41:14). |

## 4. Hinneni — "Here I Am" vs. "I'm Ready" (v. 13)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Here I am" | The classic hinneni rendering; theologically loaded |
| LOGOS | "Here I am" | Same; echoes Abraham at Moriah |
| PNEUMA | "I'm ready" | Dynamic; captures the readiness sense |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Here I am" — 2-1** | Hinneni is the Abrahamic word of obedient readiness. Joseph echoes his great-grandfather. "I'm ready" loses the echo. |

## 5. Medanites vs. Midianites (v. 36)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Medanites" (מְדָנִים) | Follows the Leningrad Codex precisely |
| LOGOS | "Midianites" | Harmonizes with v. 28 |
| PNEUMA | "Midianites" | Harmonizes with v. 28 |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Medanites" — EMET upheld** | The LC clearly reads Medanim, not Midyanim. Medan and Midian are distinct sons of Abraham (Gen 25:2). The textual distinction is part of the complex Ishmaelite/Midianite/Medanite layering that is a literary feature of this narrative. |

## 6. Na'ar — "Youth" vs. "Young Man" vs. "Assistant" (v. 2)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he was a youth" | Na'ar = youth/lad |
| LOGOS | "he was a young man" | Na'ar as age descriptor |
| PNEUMA | "He was an assistant" | Na'ar as role descriptor (servant/helper) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"He was a youth" — EMET selected** | Na'ar primarily denotes age/status in this context. Joseph is seventeen. The term marks his junior position rather than a specific occupational role. |

---

*Genesis 37: 6 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET on 3 items (vv. 2, 19/pit, 36), with EMET/LOGOS jointly on 2 items (vv. 13, 19), and established a new convention for 1 item (ketonet passim). PNEUMA was overruled on 5 items (vv. 2, 13, 19, 24, 36). The ketonet passim convention is the most significant new ruling — it parallels the massebah precedent of preserving genuinely uncertain Hebrew terms with a bracketed gloss.*
